summary: Audited the full 18-chapter, 52 289-word manuscript for cross-chapter redundancy and consistency. REDUNDANCY: The dominant pattern is a repeated examination-scene template (sleeve-roll → Mark visual → palm-on-forearm) that recurs across Ch 3, 4, 6, 7, and 8 with near-synonymous phrasing for the same physical features — the 'skull and serpent' phrase alone appears 8 times across 7 chapters. The library receives three full-inventory descriptions within two chapters (Ch 2–3). Hermione's glamour/scar morning ritual is re-established in Ch 5 after thorough Ch 1 treatment. Ch 13 re-explains the infrastructure-decay premise to Ron using information the reader has had since Ch 1. The hedge word 'something' appears 83 times manuscript-wide (1 per 630 words, threshold 1 per 800), with 7 chapters materially above threshold. Frost imagery clusters at 14 instances across 6 chapters, concentrated in the Ch 6–8 band. Em-dash density (1.8%), sentence-opener monotony (no pronoun above 7%), and near-verbatim passages all pass. CONSISTENCY: One geographic contradiction found — Narcissa's residence is 'Bath' in Ch 2 but 'Knightsbridge' in Ch 7 and the continuity sheet. All other character details, timeline, spatial continuity, objects, knowledge states, and world rules are internally consistent.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 2 line 162: 'Two stories of shelves climbed the walls. A mezzanine ran the perimeter, fitted with rolling ladders and iron rails polished by hands long dead… old paper, dust, preservation charms worked so deeply into the bindings that the air itself seemed lacquered'; Ch 2 line 296: 'shelves rising to the ceiling, mezzanine circling overhead, centuries of collection and theft and scholarship'; Ch 3 line 433: 'Two stories of shelved walls rose around a mezzanine gallery. Rolling ladders clung to brass tracks. Reading tables sat in long shafts of autumn light. Old paper and dust held the air, with preservation charms underneath, mineral and faintly metallic'
redundancy_findings.problem: The library receives its full establishment in Ch 2 (line 162) and a second confirming view during Hermione's guided tour (line 296). Ch 3 line 433 then re-inventories the same space a third time with near-identical elements: two-story shelves, mezzanine, rolling ladders, old paper/dust/preservation-charm atmosphere. A reader arriving at Ch 3 one chapter after Ch 2 already knows all of these details.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut or radically compress the Ch 3 library description at line 433. Enter the space through what is NEW for Hermione as a working researcher — the chaos of the collection, the specific texts she discovers. The passage beginning at line 435 ('The southeast corner was arbitrary…') already does this well; let it carry the scene entry.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 3's library entry passage no longer mentions mezzanine, rolling ladders, two-story shelves, or the old-paper/preservation-charm atmosphere. The reader learns something about the space they did not already know from Ch 2.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 3 line 363: 'he sat with his left sleeve rolled to the elbow and his forearm laid flat between them like evidence entered into a file'; Ch 3 lines 375-377: 'faded toward dark grey… skull and serpent curved with the arm's actual lines, ink laid over tendon and bone'; Ch 4 line 542: 'With his sleeve rolled up, pale forearm bare, he looked down at the skull and serpent and saw that time had done nothing to it'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 3 (lines 363, 375-377) establishes the sleeve-rolling posture and provides the first detailed visual of the Mark (dark grey, faded, skull and serpent over tendon and bone). Ch 4 (line 542), the immediately following chapter, re-describes the identical gesture and feature: sleeve rolled up, forearm bare, skull and serpent.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch 4 line 542, enter the Mark scene through Draco's somatic experience (the ache, the heat, the Riddle House memory) rather than re-describing the visual. Keep 'the Mark had been bad overnight and worse at dawn'; cut the sleeve-up visual and 'skull and serpent.'
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 4's Mark passage no longer describes the sleeve-rolling gesture or the skull-and-serpent visual. The scene is anchored in pain and memory rather than repeated visual inventory.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 1 line 43: 'Her left forearm itched. Automatically, she tugged her sleeve lower. The glamour held… Seven years of repetition had made the sequence part of dressing: wake up, shower, charm the forearm, choose a shirt, leave.'; Ch 5 line 701: 'After a quick pass of her wand over the glamour on her left forearm, smoothing scar tissue into anonymous skin, Hermione dressed and took the walk to the library.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 1 (line 43) thoroughly establishes Hermione's morning glamour ritual — the itch, the sleeve tug, the daily check, the seven-year dressing sequence. Ch 5 (line 701) re-describes the same ritual: wand over glamour, left forearm, scar tissue smoothed, then dressing. The reader does not need the mechanism explained again.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch 5 line 701, compress the glamour-ritual clause to a single beat ('After the usual morning sequence' or a brief tactile check) and enter through the new detail — the house angling the east-facing window to catch dawn.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 5's morning passage does not re-explain the glamour mechanism, scar tissue, or forearm. The reader's Ch 1 knowledge is treated as established.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 6 line 1084: 'He rolled his left sleeve up. The Mark stood dark against his skin, the lines raised enough to catch the light.'; Ch 7 line 1491: 'Left sleeve pushed to the elbow. The Mark sat dark on his forearm, snake and skull sharply inked against skin that almost never saw sun.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 7 (line 1491) re-describes the sleeve-rolling and Mark in near-synonymous terms to Ch 6 (line 1084): sleeve rolled/pushed to elbow, Mark dark against/on skin. These are adjacent chapters. This is the 4th instance of the sleeve-plus-Mark-visual template after Ch 3, 4, and 6.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch 7 line 1491, cut the sleeve-and-Mark visual entirely. The scene's real content is Hermione watching him write — keep the left-handedness observation and the mismatch between his handwriting and the Mark. Enter the Mark through sensation or narrative consequence.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 7 line 1491 no longer describes the sleeve position, the Mark's darkness, or the skull-and-serpent visual. The Mark is present through its effect, not visual re-inventory.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 8 line 1614: 'Across from her, Draco rolled his left sleeve to the elbow.'; Ch 8 line 1616: 'Routine did not make the skull and serpent less ugly. It only made the sight of it survivable.'; earlier instances at Ch 3 line 363, Ch 4 line 542, Ch 6 line 1084, Ch 7 line 1491
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 8 (lines 1614-1616) is the 5th instance of the sleeve-roll-plus-Mark-description template. The text acknowledges the repetition ('Now it was routine') but still re-delivers the skull-and-serpent image. The acknowledgment of routine does not erase the reader's experience of redundancy.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Let the Ch 8 examination open from the diagnostic work or emotional state rather than the sleeve-rolling gesture. If the routine must be referenced, one compressed clause is sufficient. Cut 'skull and serpent' — the reader has encountered it five times.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 8's examination opening does not contain 'skull and serpent' or a full sleeve-rolling sequence. The scene begins in diagnostic action or emotional state.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 2 line 162: 'Two stories of shelves climbed the walls… rolling ladders and iron rails'; Ch 3 line 433: 'Rolling ladders clung to brass tracks'; Ch 7 line 1497: 'past shelves that climbed to the ceiling and rolling ladders Hermione now used with a recklessness that would once have alarmed her in a student.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 7 (line 1497) re-describes the library's shelves-and-ladders for the reader even though these elements were established in Ch 2 (line 162) and re-established in Ch 3 (line 433). The character beat (Hermione's reckless ladder use) is new, but it is wrapped in setting re-description the reader does not need.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep the character beat about reckless ladder use but cut the setting inventory. Replace 'past shelves that climbed to the ceiling and rolling ladders' with a compressed reference ('through the stacks') and foreground the recklessness detail.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 7 line 1497 no longer re-describes the library's height, shelving, or ladders. The passage moves through the space without re-orienting the reader.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 13 lines 2671-2735: Hermione explains to Ron: 'The ambient field's destabilizing… The infrastructure that regulates baseline magical pressure has degraded… There's material in the Malfoy library that documents the original magical infrastructure. The anchoring points, the ley-line connections, the deep structure…'; established in Ch 1 lines 7-139 (multiple infrastructure failures), Ch 5 lines 911-1037 (Floo hub degradation), Ch 7 lines 1337+ (research points to deep infrastructure)
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 13 contains an exposition block (lines 2671-2735) where Hermione explains the infrastructure-decay premise, the Malfoy library's importance, and the anchoring-point theory to Ron. The reader has had this information since Ch 1 (Floo failures, charm failures), Ch 5 (hub-level degradation), and Ch 7 (anchoring theory). While Ron needs the information within the story, the current passage re-teaches the reader at length rather than compressing what is already known.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Compress the exposition to Ron. Let Hermione summarize in 2-3 sentences rather than re-explaining the full chain (ambient field → infrastructure → anchoring points → Malfoy records). The reader needs only the NEW information: scale (children, Hogwarts wards, Azkaban). Cut re-statements of premise the reader already holds.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 13's explanation to Ron no longer re-teaches the ambient-field-to-infrastructure chain. The scene conveys Hermione's urgency and Ron's reaction without repeating the reader's existing knowledge at expository length.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 2 lines 156-157: kitchen as solitary space ('one mug on the rack… lone plate drying beside it'); Ch 10 lines 1956-1962: 'The kitchen was the oldest part of the Manor that still worked like a kitchen: stone floor, scarred counters, a kettle that had sat on the same stretch of counter since before his grandfather's time'; Ch 10 line 1958: 'Better to manage a manual flame than explain to Granger that his household magic was failing along with everyone else's'
redundancy_findings.problem: Draco's isolation in the Manor kitchen is established in Ch 2 (single mug, lone plate, eating standing because choosing one chair means seeing the other three empty). Ch 10 (line 1956-1962) re-proves the same baseline — solitary kitchen use, failing domestic magic — without materially new leverage or consequence. The failing kettle is a new symptom, but the emotional note (one person in a space meant for more) duplicates Ch 2.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Enter the Ch 10 kitchen scene through the new element (the enchanted kettle failing, his choice to hide the failure from Granger) rather than re-describing the kitchen's age, surfaces, and solitary atmosphere. The reader knows this kitchen; show what has changed.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 10's kitchen opening does not re-inventory the stone floor, counters, or single-occupant atmosphere. The scene enters through the kettle failure and the concealment motive.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 8 contains 4 of 14 total 'frost' instances in a 1707-word chapter (lines 1604, 1632, 1636, 1640+). Other frost-heavy chapters: Ch 6 (4 instances in 2929 words), Ch 7 (2), Ch 12 (2). Total manuscript: 14 instances across 6 chapters, concentrated in the Ch 6-8 band.
redundancy_findings.problem: 'Frost' appears 14 times across the manuscript, with 10 of those instances packed into the consecutive Ch 6–8 band. Ch 8 alone has 4 instances in 1707 words — one every ~430 words. Target: keep the strongest 4-6 instances manuscript-wide. The Ch 8 concentration turns a seasonal detail into an ambient refrain.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch 8, keep at most one frost reference (the opening 'frost still clung to the east window' at line 1604 is the strongest). Replace the remaining instances with varied sensory detail — cold light, condensation, thin air — or cut them. The reader knows it is December in a stone house.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 8 contains at most one instance of 'frost.' The December atmosphere is conveyed through varied sensory language rather than a single repeated noun.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 6 contains 4 of 14 total 'frost' instances in 2929 words (lines 1080, 1098+, 1136+, 1182+). Combined with Ch 7 (2) and Ch 8 (4), the Ch 6-8 band accounts for 10 of 14 manuscript-wide frost instances.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 6 has 4 frost references in 2929 words. Together with the adjacent Ch 7 (2) and Ch 8 (4), the reader encounters frost 10 times in three consecutive chapters. Target: keep 4-6 instances manuscript-wide. The Ch 6 cluster should retain 1-2 and cede the rest.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch 6, keep the strongest frost image (likely line 1080 'Frost feathered the lower panes'). Replace or cut the other three instances. Vary the winter-sensory palette: wind, thin light, breath visible, or temperature against skin.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 6 contains at most 2 instances of 'frost.' Winter atmosphere uses at least two different sensory details.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'something' appears 5 times in Ch 11's 1538 prose words (1 per 308 words). Manuscript-wide: 83 occurrences / 52289 words = 1 per 630 words. Threshold: 1 per 800 words (65 max). Ch 11 rate is 2.6x the threshold.
redundancy_findings.problem: The hedge word 'something' exceeds the 1-per-800-words threshold manuscript-wide (83 total, 1 per 630). Ch 11 is the single worst contributor at 1 per 308 words — 2.6 times the threshold. Each 'something' defers a specific image or sensation the prose could name directly.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Review each of the 5 'something' instances in Ch 11. Replace at least 3 with the specific noun, sensation, or action the hedge is deferring. Keep at most 2 where genuine uncertainty is the point.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 11 contains at most 2 instances of 'something.' Replaced instances name the specific thing that was hedged.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'something' appears 7 times in Ch 16's 2587 prose words (1 per 370 words). Manuscript-wide: 83/52289 = 1 per 630 words. Threshold: 1 per 800.
redundancy_findings.problem: 'something' in Ch 16 runs at 1 per 370 words — 2.2 times the threshold. Seven hedges in a single chapter erode specificity during a climactic confrontation chapter that needs maximum precision.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Review each of the 7 instances. In a confrontation chapter, precision matters most. Replace at least 4 with the specific emotion, object, or realization being deferred.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 16 contains at most 3 instances of 'something.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'something' appears 9 times in Ch 18's 3367 prose words (1 per 374 words). Manuscript-wide: 83/52289 = 1 per 630. Threshold: 1 per 800.
redundancy_findings.problem: 'something' in Ch 18 runs at 1 per 374 words — 2.1 times the threshold. Nine hedges in the final chapter blunt the resolution and reconciliation beats that the entire novel builds toward.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Review each of the 9 instances. Replace at least 5 with the specific noun or sensation. The closing chapter should name what earlier chapters deferred.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 18 contains at most 4 instances of 'something.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'something' appears 10 times in Ch 5's 3999 prose words (1 per 400 words). Manuscript-wide: 83/52289 = 1 per 630. Threshold: 1 per 800.
redundancy_findings.problem: 'something' in Ch 5 runs at 1 per 400 words — twice the threshold. Ten hedges in a chapter centered on intellectual intimacy and the emerging Mark resonance undercut the precision the prose claims for both characters.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Review each of the 10 instances. Replace at least 6 with specific nouns or sensations, particularly in passages about the Mark resonance and the research rhythm.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 5 contains at most 4 instances of 'something.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'something' appears 11 times in Ch 13's 4845 prose words (1 per 441 words). Manuscript-wide: 83/52289 = 1 per 630. Threshold: 1 per 800.
redundancy_findings.problem: 'something' in Ch 13 runs at 1 per 441 words — 1.8 times the threshold. Eleven hedges in a chapter that includes the Harry-at-work scene and the Ron pub meeting dilute both conversations.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Review each of the 11 instances. Replace at least 6 with specific nouns or emotional states. Conversation chapters benefit most from precision.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 13 contains at most 5 instances of 'something.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'something' appears 8 times in Ch 15's 3814 prose words (1 per 477 words). Manuscript-wide: 83/52289 = 1 per 630. Threshold: 1 per 800.
redundancy_findings.problem: 'something' in Ch 15 runs at 1 per 477 words — 1.7 times the threshold. The chapter contains the first kiss scene, where hedged language ('something that was simultaneously desired and suspect') undermines a climactic beat.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Review each of the 8 instances. Replace at least 4, prioritizing the kiss scene and the research-breakthrough passages where specificity strengthens the moment.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 15 contains at most 4 instances of 'something.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'something' appears 7 times in Ch 7's 3491 prose words (1 per 499 words). Manuscript-wide: 83/52289 = 1 per 630. Threshold: 1 per 800.
redundancy_findings.problem: 'something' in Ch 7 runs at 1 per 499 words — 1.6 times the threshold. The Narcissa-Hermione conversation is a precision scene where vagueness weakens Narcissa's carefully oblique disclosures.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Review each of the 7 instances. Replace at least 3, particularly in Narcissa's dialogue and the research-progress exposition.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 7 contains at most 4 instances of 'something.'

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch 2 line 172: 'Three years earlier she had moved into a townhouse in Bath, tasteful and proportionate, rooms that stayed where they were put.'; Ch 7 line 1445: 'I live in a rented house in Knightsbridge because the Ministry cannot decide whether my war service was treason or not'; Continuity sheet line 114: 'Lives apart from the Manor in a rented house in Knightsbridge'
consistency_findings.problem: Narcissa's residence is stated as 'Bath' in Ch 2 (line 172) but 'Knightsbridge' in Ch 7 (line 1445). The continuity sheet confirms Knightsbridge as canonical. The Ch 2 reference is the error.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch 2 line 172, change 'a townhouse in Bath' to 'a rented house in Knightsbridge' (or equivalent London phrasing consistent with Ch 7's self-description). No other changes needed — the characterization ('tasteful and proportionate, rooms that stayed where they were put') works for either location.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 2's reference to Narcissa's residence matches 'Knightsbridge' as stated in Ch 7 and the continuity sheet. No other location name for her home appears in the manuscript.
